content.text: Whisper (OpenAI, 2022) is a weakly supervised encoder-decoder Transformer trained on 680,000 hours of internet audio. It is the dominant open-source ASR system for most use cases: it covers 99 languages, supports both transcription and translation to English, produces word-level timestamps, and achieves near-human accuracy on well-recorded English speech without any fine-tuning. Understanding Whisper's architecture, conditioning token scheme, model variants, and practical deployment patterns equips you to use it correctly and to know when its limitations matter.

attrs.alt: Whisper architecture: raw audio -> log-mel (80x3000) -> Conv stem -> Transformer encoder (1500 hidden states) -> cross-attended by Transformer decoder. Decoder conditioning prefix: SOT, language tag, task tag, timestamp flag, then BPE text tokens interleaved with timestamp tokens, ending at EOT. Bottom panels show training data composition, output modes, and language coverage.

content.text: The encoder processes a fixed 30-second audio clip. Audio shorter than 30 seconds is zero-padded on the right. The STFT is computed with a 400-sample Hann window (25 ms at 16 kHz) and 160-sample hop (10 ms), producing 3,000 frames of 80-bin log-mel features. A two-layer 1D convolutional stem with GELU activations and stride 2 on the second layer compresses this to 1,500 time steps with the model's hidden dimension (512 for small, 1024 for large). These 1,500 vectors become the encoder's key-value pairs for the decoder's cross-attention.

The decoder is a standard autoregressive Transformer with learned positional embeddings. A key design choice is the conditioning prefix — a sequence of special tokens that precedes the actual transcript tokens and controls the model's behaviour without any architectural change.

Every Whisper output begins with a fixed prefix of special tokens before any transcript text. The prefix encodes the task, language, and timestamp behaviour. This single-model multi-task design means all capabilities are accessed by modifying the prefix, not by switching models:
Always first; signals start of generation
Language tag — one of 99 language codes; detected or forced
Task: transcribe audio in source language. Use
Task: translate audio to English, regardless of source language
Suppresses timestamp tokens; output is plain text only
Starting timestamp: model emits timestamp tokens between words

When timestamps are enabled, the model interleaves timestamp tokens (in 20 ms increments from 0.00 to 30.00 s) with BPE text tokens. These provide segment-level boundaries in the standard API and word-level boundaries when the WhisperX force-aligner is applied post-hoc.
Training Data and Weakly Supervised Learning

Whisper's 680,000-hour training corpus was collected from the internet by matching audio to existing subtitles and captions — not by paying human transcribers. This weakly supervised approach scales data collection by several orders of magnitude compared to manually transcribed corpora (LibriSpeech has 960 hours; Common Voice has ~16,000 hours).

The label quality varies: subtitles are typically accurate for formally produced media (podcasts, YouTube lectures, news broadcasts) but misaligned or incorrect for auto-captioned content. OpenAI applied heuristic filters to remove low-quality pairs (high character-to-word ratio, non-speech audio, music-heavy clips) but did not remove all noise. As a result, Whisper has been observed to hallucinate text in silence or music segments — it learned to always produce output rather than to recognise when to output nothing.
of English transcription audio. Roughly 98,000 hours of this is machine-generated labels (auto-captions); the remainder is human-created subtitles.
of non-English audio with transcriptions in the source language, spanning 96 languages. Mandarin, Spanish, Portuguese, German, and French have the most data.
of audio-to-English translation training data: the task token switches to
and the labels are English translations of foreign-language audio.

Whisper is released in five sizes plus the turbo variant introduced in 2024. Each variant doubles the parameter count from the previous. The encoder-decoder architecture is identical across all sizes; only the number of layers, heads, and hidden dimension change.
Good balance for most EN applications

The turbo model uses a distilled decoder with only 2 layers (down from 32) while keeping the full 32-layer encoder. Because encoder computation dominates at inference (the encoder processes 1,500 frames vs. the decoder's short transcript), this asymmetric reduction achieves ~8x speedup with minimal accuracy loss. Turbo is generally the best choice for throughput-sensitive deployments where large-v3 is too slow.

When no language is forced, Whisper detects the language by running the encoder on the first 30-second chunk, then performing a restricted decode that generates only a language token. The logits over all language tokens are converted to probabilities and the highest-probability language is selected. The detection is exposed via the API:

import whisper
import torch

model = whisper.load_model("turbo")

# Detect language without transcribing
audio = whisper.load_audio("speech.wav")
audio_padded = whisper.pad_or_trim(audio)
mel = whisper.log_mel_spectrogram(audio_padded).to(model.device)

_, probs = model.detect_language(mel)
detected = max(probs, key=probs.get)
print(f"Language: {detected}, probability: {probs[detected]:.2%}")

# Transcribe with automatic language detection
result = model.transcribe("speech.wav")
print(f"Detected: {result['language']}")
print(result['text'])

The official OpenAI Whisper library uses PyTorch and runs beam search in Python, leaving significant throughput on the table. faster-whisper re-implements the model with CTranslate2 — a high-performance inference framework for sequence models — applying INT8 weight quantisation and optimised CUDA kernels. The result is 4–8x higher throughput with no accuracy regression on standard benchmarks.

from faster_whisper import WhisperModel

# INT8 on GPU: 4-8x faster than original whisper
model = WhisperModel("large-v3", device="cuda", compute_type="int8")

# INT8 on CPU: still competitive with whisper-small on GPU
model_cpu = WhisperModel("medium", device="cpu", compute_type="int8")

# Transcribe with VAD chunking, word timestamps
segments, info = model.transcribe(
    "audio.mp3",
    beam_size=5,
    language="en",             # optional: skip auto-detection
    vad_filter=True,           # chunk at silence boundaries
    word_timestamps=True,
    condition_on_previous_text=False  # disable for noisy/music audio
)

print(f"Language: {info.language} ({info.language_probability:.0%})")
for seg in segments:
    for word in seg.words:
        print(f"  [{word.start:.2f}s-{word.end:.2f}s] {word.word}")

For processing large archives of audio files, batching multiple audio clips into a single forward pass maximises GPU utilisation. WhisperX supports batched inference where multiple 30-second chunks are processed simultaneously:

import whisperx

# WhisperX batched transcription (much faster for archives)
model = whisperx.load_model(
    "large-v3",
    device="cuda",
    compute_type="float16",
    asr_options={"suppress_numerals": True}
)

audio = whisperx.load_audio("long_audio.mp3")

# batch_size=16 means 16 chunks of 30s processed in parallel
result = model.transcribe(audio, batch_size=16, language="en")

# Force-align for word-level timestamps
align_model, metadata = whisperx.load_align_model(language_code="en", device="cuda")
result = whisperx.align(result["segments"], align_model, metadata, audio, device="cuda")

for segment in result["segments"]:
    words = [(w["word"], w["start"], w["end"]) for w in segment["words"]]
    print(words)
Whisper hallucinates — it generates plausible-sounding text that was not in the audio. Hallucination occurs most often in:

Whisper was never trained on audio paired with empty transcripts. When the audio contains only background noise or music, it may generate filler text from its training distribution rather than producing nothing.
The end of a long transcript.

With condition_on_previous_text=True (default), Whisper feeds its previous output as a prefix when chunking long audio. If an earlier chunk contains an error, later chunks may hallucinate text consistent with that error rather than what is actually spoken.
Languages with under 1,000 training hours may produce transcripts in the wrong language or hallucinate common English phrases because the model over-relies on its dominant English training signal.
to suppress output when the model's no-speech probability exceeds 60%. Apply VAD filtering before transcription so silent segments are never passed to the encoder.

Whisper's base training does not specialise for narrow domains. Fine-tuning on domain-specific data reduces WER by 30–60% on in-domain test sets. The training setup uses the standard Hugging Face Seq2Seq pipeline:

from transformers import WhisperForConditionalGeneration, WhisperProcessor
from transformers import Seq2SeqTrainer, Seq2SeqTrainingArguments
from datasets import Dataset
import torch

processor = WhisperProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/whisper-small", language="English", task="transcribe")
model = WhisperForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained("openai/whisper-small")
model.config.forced_decoder_ids = None
model.config.suppress_tokens = []

def prepare_batch(batch):
    # batch["audio"] is a dict with {"array": np.ndarray, "sampling_rate": int}
    features = processor.feature_extractor(
        batch["audio"]["array"],
        sampling_rate=batch["audio"]["sampling_rate"]
    )
    batch["input_features"] = features.input_features[0]
    labels = processor.tokenizer(batch["transcript"]).input_ids
    # replace padding token id with -100 so loss ignores padding
    batch["labels"] = [-100 if t == processor.tokenizer.pad_token_id else t for t in labels]
    return batch

args = Seq2SeqTrainingArguments(
    output_dir="whisper-domain",
    num_train_epochs=5,
    per_device_train_batch_size=16,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=1,
    learning_rate=1e-5,
    warmup_steps=100,
    fp16=True,
    predict_with_generate=True,
    generation_max_length=225,
    save_steps=500,
    eval_steps=500,
    logging_steps=50,
    report_to=["tensorboard"]
)
# trainer.train() proceeds as usual

When fine-tuning on small domain datasets (under 5 hours), freeze the encoder and only fine-tune the decoder. The encoder's learned acoustic representations generalise well; overfitting the encoder to a small corpus degrades multilingual and non-domain performance.
WhisperX: Forced Alignment for Word-Level Timestamps

Whisper's built-in word timestamps are approximate: they come from the decoder's timestamp token predictions, which can be off by 100–300 ms on individual words. WhisperX improves this by applying phoneme-level forced alignment (using wav2vec 2.0 as the alignment model) after transcription, producing precise word-level boundaries within ~20 ms of the true boundary.
The forced aligner maps each word in the Whisper transcript to the audio waveform by:
Phonemising each word using a G2P model.
Running wav2vec 2.0 over the audio segment to produce frame-level phoneme posteriors.
Using CTC dynamic programming (Viterbi path) to find the optimal monotone alignment between phoneme sequence and audio frames.
Collapsing phoneme boundaries to word boundaries by grouping consecutive phonemes of the same word.
WhisperX alignment models are available for 35 languages. For languages not covered, the pipeline falls back to Whisper's native timestamp tokens.

OpenAI exposes Whisper through its cloud API, allowing transcription without self-hosting the model. The API is priced per minute of audio and supports files up to 25 MB (approximately 25 minutes at 128 kbps MP3). For larger files, split before sending:

from openai import OpenAI
import math
from pydub import AudioSegment

client = OpenAI()  # reads OPENAI_API_KEY from environment

# Transcribe a short audio file (under 25MB)
with open("speech.mp3", "rb") as f:
    transcript = client.audio.transcriptions.create(
        model="whisper-1",
        file=f,
        response_format="verbose_json",   # includes segments with timestamps
        language="en"                      # optional: force language
    )
print(transcript.text)

# Transcribe a long file by chunking
def transcribe_long_audio(file_path, chunk_minutes=15):
    audio = AudioSegment.from_file(file_path)
    chunk_ms = chunk_minutes * 60 * 1000
    chunks = math.ceil(len(audio) / chunk_ms)
    full_text = []
    for i in range(chunks):
        chunk = audio[i * chunk_ms : (i + 1) * chunk_ms]
        chunk_path = f"/tmp/chunk_{i}.mp3"
        chunk.export(chunk_path, format="mp3", bitrate="128k")
        with open(chunk_path, "rb") as f:
            result = client.audio.transcriptions.create(
                model="whisper-1", file=f, response_format="text"
            )
        full_text.append(result)
    return " ".join(full_text)
Whisper is not the right tool for every ASR scenario. Understand its limitations before committing to it:
Whisper processes 30s chunks; 30s minimum initial latency
WER often 30%+; risk of wrong-language output
Fine-tune Whisper on accented data, or wav2vec 2.0
Audio with music or TV audio in background
Separate speech from background first (demucs, Spleeter)
Cloud API sends audio to OpenAI servers
Self-host faster-whisper; do not use the cloud API

Whisper accepts an optional initial_prompt parameter that prepends text to the decoder context before transcription begins. This is distinct from the conditioning tokens and allows you to bias the model toward a specific vocabulary, spelling, or style:

import whisper

model = whisper.load_model("small")

# Use initial_prompt to bias vocabulary
# Useful for proper nouns, product names, or specialised jargon
result = model.transcribe(
    "medical_dictation.wav",
    initial_prompt="This is a medical consultation about hypertension, "
                   "metoprolol, and ACE inhibitors."
)

# Result: the model is more likely to produce correct medical terms
# because the prompt primes the decoder context
print(result["text"])

# For the OpenAI API:
with open("medical_dictation.wav", "rb") as f:
    result = client.audio.transcriptions.create(
        model="whisper-1",
        file=f,
        prompt="Medical consultation. Patient reports chest pain, dyspnea, "
               "and tachycardia. Physician prescribes atenolol 25mg."
    )

The initial prompt works because the model treats it as the previous segment's text when condition_on_previous_text=True. The decoder's self-attention sees the prompt as prior context and assigns higher probability to continuations that use similar vocabulary. Use it to:
Enforce correct spelling of proper nouns (product names, person names, company names).
Prime a specific writing style (e.g., always spell out numbers in legal transcription).
Suppress hallucination by providing the correct context for the first segment.
Do not use the initial prompt as a security boundary: Whisper will discard the prompt entirely if it cannot be naturally continued by the actual audio content. It is a bias, not a hard constraint.
Before deploying Whisper in a production pipeline, measure WER on a representative sample of your actual audio. The process:

from jiwer import wer, cer
import whisper

model = whisper.load_model("turbo")

# Evaluate WER on a test set
references = [
    "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog",
    "machine learning is transforming speech recognition"
]
audio_files = ["audio1.wav", "audio2.wav"]

hypotheses = []
for audio_path in audio_files:
    result = model.transcribe(audio_path)
    hypotheses.append(result["text"].lower().strip())

# jiwer handles tokenisation and edit distance computation
word_error_rate = wer(references, hypotheses)
char_error_rate = cer(references, hypotheses)
print(f"WER: {word_error_rate:.1%}")
print(f"CER: {char_error_rate:.1%}")

# Common post-processing fixes
import re
def normalise(text):
    text = text.lower().strip()
    text = re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9 ']", "", text)   # remove punctuation for WER
    text = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text)           # collapse whitespace
    return text

Before computing WER, apply text normalisation to both hypothesis and reference: lowercasing, punctuation removal, number expansion (1 -> one), and whitespace collapsing. The Whisper normaliser (whisper.normalizers.BasicTextNormalizer for non-English) handles these consistently.
Whisper's conditioning token prefix controls all behaviour (language, task, timestamps) without architecture changes — the same model transcribes, translates, and timestamps.
The turbo model offers 8x speedup over large-v3 by distilling the decoder to 2 layers while keeping the full 32-layer encoder — the best throughput/quality trade-off for most production deployments.
faster-whisper with INT8 quantisation is 4–8x faster than the original Python implementation and is the standard production choice.
Hallucination is a real issue in silence, low-resource languages, and long audio with conditioning on previous text — mitigate with VAD filtering and no_speech_threshold.
Fine-tuning on domain-specific data reduces WER by 30–60% relative; freeze the encoder when fine-tuning data is under 5 hours.
